commonProblems.h2: BMW M6 Engine Problems - What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: The BMW M6’s S85 V10 and S63 V8 twin-turbo engines deliver breathtaking performance, but both have well-documented weak points that drive replacement demand among UK owners. The S85 dominates earlier E63/E64 generation failure searches, while S63 issues-turbo failures, oil consumption, and cooling system problems-define F06/F12/F13 ownership. Every rebuilt engine supplied through EnginesMarket includes upgraded internal components designed to address the original failure point, plus a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty. Below, we break down the most common M6 engine failures by cost, repair tier, and whether replacement makes more sense than repair.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: What It Involves
repairOptions.longevity: Longevity / Suitability

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace rod bearings, bolts, oil pump bolts, sump gasket, oil service
repairOptions.longevity: 60k-80k miles; suitable for any M6 without existing knock

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: As above plus crankshaft removal, inspection, and polishing
repairOptions.longevity: 30k-60k miles; viable if damage caught early without scoring

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete S85 replacement engine with upgraded bearings, bench tested, 12-month warranty
repairOptions.longevity: 60k+ mile lifespan; only viable option if crank is scored or rods damaged

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: What It Involves
repairOptions.longevity: Longevity / Suitability

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace failed actuator (bank 1 or 2) with OEM or uprated unit
repairOptions.longevity: 30k-50k miles; but the other bank will likely fail soon after

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace both throttle actuators with uprated units with upgraded gears and electronics
repairOptions.longevity: 80k+ miles; uprated units often carry lifetime warranty

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Engine Swap (if secondary damage occurs)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete S85 engine replacement if prolonged limp mode driving caused internal damage
repairOptions.longevity: Only if engine was driven hard in limp mode for extended period

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: What It Involves
repairOptions.longevity: Longevity / Suitability

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace failed turbo, oil feed lines, gaskets
repairOptions.longevity: 40k-60k miles; but labour overlaps make doing both at once more economical

repairOptions.tier: Full Repair - Both Turbos Replaced + Oil System Flush
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace both turbos, oil feed/return lines, oil cooler flush, fresh oil service
repairOptions.longevity: 60k-80k miles; the recommended approach for high-mileage S63s

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete S63 replacement engine with upgraded turbos and cooling components
repairOptions.longevity: Only if turbo failure caused metal contamination throughout the engine

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: What It Involves
repairOptions.longevity: Longevity / Suitability

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace all 32 valve stem seals (can be done with heads on using specialised tooling)
repairOptions.longevity: 40k-60k miles; effective if rings are still healthy

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Full top-end rebuild with new rings, seals, timing components
repairOptions.longevity: 60k-80k miles; addresses both oil consumption sources

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete S63 replacement engine with revised rings and seals
repairOptions.longevity: 60k+ mile lifespan; often more cost-effective than major rebuild on high-mileage engines

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: What It Involves
repairOptions.longevity: Longevity / Suitability

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace failed hose/connector, bleed cooling system
repairOptions.longevity: 2-3 years; but other aged hoses will likely fail soon

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace water pump, thermostat, all coolant hoses, expansion tank, coolant
repairOptions.longevity: 5+ years; recommended preventative maintenance at 70k miles

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete S63 replacement engine following overheating damage
repairOptions.longevity: Only if catastrophic overheating caused head gasket failure or bore damage

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: What It Involves
repairOptions.longevity: Longevity / Suitability

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Remove, clean or replace VANOS solenoids, fresh oil service
repairOptions.longevity: 30k-50k miles; addresses VANOS fault codes if caught early

repairOptions.tier: Intermediate - Full VANOS Seal & Hub Repair
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace all VANOS seals, hubs (S85 bolt upgrade), solenoids, timing chain tensioner
repairOptions.longevity: 60k+ miles; essential preventative work on high-mileage S85

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Rebuilt Engine (if hub bolts fail)
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete engine replacement following valve/piston contact
repairOptions.longevity: Only if VANOS hub failure caused internal collision damage

engineYears.h2: BMW M6 Engines by Year - What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This section maps every major engine change across the BMW M6 production timeline from 1983 to 2018, helping owners identify their exact unit and compare rebuilt engine prices from UK specialists.

years.description: The E24 M635CSi launched BMW M's grand tourer lineage with a hand-built M88/3 straight-six - a low-volume halo car that established the M6 name.
keyChanges: M635CSi introduced as the high-performance variant of the E24 6 Series
M88/3 engine hand-assembled at BMW M's Garching factory
Limited UK supply with most cars remaining in left-hand drive
Production ceased in 1989 with no direct successor until 2005
knownFor: Individual throttle bodies and mechanical fuel injection
Hand-built M-division engine with 286hp - formidable for its era
Timing chain tensioner wear on higher-mileage examples
Rising classic values mean rebuilds are increasingly specialist-led

years.preview: E63/E64 M6 Launch: The S85 V10 Arrives
years.description: BMW stunned the automotive world by fitting a 5.0-litre V10 derived from the Sauber F1 programme into the E63/E64 M6 - the only production V10 ever fitted to a BMW M car.
keyChanges: All-new M6 Coupe (E63) and Convertible (E64) launched
S85B50 V10 produced 507hp with an 8,250rpm redline
Seven-speed SMG III gearbox as the only transmission option
Carbon-fibre roof debuted on Coupe models
knownFor: F1-inspired V10 with individual throttle bodies and ion-sense knock detection
Rod bearing failures documented from as early as 50,000 miles
Throttle actuator gear failure triggering limp mode (fault codes 2B15/2B16)
SMG pump failures are common on higher-mileage cars
years.cta: Get quotes for 2005-2006 BMW M6 V10 replacement engines
years.ticker: BMW M6 S85B50 engine replacement • BMW M6 rod bearing failure cost • BMW M6 V10 rebuilt engine price
enquiries: BMW M6 rod bearing failure cost
BMW M6 V10 rebuilt engine price

years.description: The 2008 facelift brought minor cosmetic updates and the revised S85B50B engine, but retained the V10's character and its well-documented maintenance demands.
keyChanges: LCI (Life Cycle Impulse) facelift with revised lights and bumpers
S85B50B engine with updated DME software
iDrive system upgraded to CIC (Car Information Computer)
Final production year for E63/E64 M6 was 2010
mainEngines: S85B50B - 5.0L Petrol (M6 Coupe LCI, M6 Convertible LCI)
knownFor: Same rod bearing weakness as pre-facelift S85B50
Throttle actuator failures remain a common replacement driver
High oil consumption between services is typical
Last naturally aspirated M6 before the switch to turbocharged V8s
years.cta: Compare 2008-2010 BMW M6 LCI V10 engine quotes
years.ticker: BMW M6 2008 engine replacement • BMW M6 2009 S85B50B price • BMW M6 V10 LCI rebuilt engine
enquiries: BMW M6 V10 LCI rebuilt engine

years.preview: F12/F13 M6 Launch: S63B44A Twin-Turbo V8
years.description: The all-new F12 Convertible and F13 Coupe (and 2013 F06 Gran Coupe) replaced the V10 with a 560hp twin-turbo V8 - more torque, lower emissions, but new failure patterns.
keyChanges: New F12 (Convertible) and F13 (Coupe) generation launched
S63B44A 4.4L twin-turbo V8 with 560hp and 500lb-ft torque
M-DCT dual-clutch transmission replaced SMG
F06 Gran Coupe four-door variant added for 2013
Electric power steering introduced
mainEngines: S63B44A - 4.4L Petrol (M6 Coupe, M6 Convertible, M6 Gran Coupe)
knownFor: Hot-vee turbo placement reduces lag but increases under-bonnet temperatures
Turbocharger bearing failures become common after 70,000 miles
Early S63 engines suffer rod bearing wear (reduced frequency versus S85)
M-DCT transmission is robust but clutch wear is costly
years.cta: Get quotes for 2012-2014 BMW M6 S63B44A replacement engines
years.ticker: BMW M6 2012 S63B44A replacement • BMW M6 2013 turbo failure cost • BMW M6 Gran Coupe engine price
enquiries: BMW M6 Gran Coupe engine price

years.description: BMW released the Competition Package for the M6 in 2014, lifting power to 575hp through revised engine mapping and improved cooling - a popular upgrade for enthusiasts.
keyChanges: Competition Package available on Coupe and Gran Coupe
S63B44C engine with 575hp (15hp increase over standard)
Revised suspension tuning and steering mapping
Unique 20-inch alloy wheels and dark chrome exhaust tips
mainEngines: S63B44C - 4.4L Petrol (M6 Coupe Competition, M6 Gran Coupe Competition)
knownFor: Turbocharger failure risk similar to standard S63B44A
Cooling system upgrades but hot-vee hose degradation remains an issue
Competition badge commands a premium on the used market
Rare in UK compared to standard M6 models
years.cta: Compare 2014-2015 BMW M6 Competition Package engine prices

years.description: The 2015 LCI brought the revised S63B44B with Valvetronic and later the 600hp S63T2 - the most powerful and durable iteration of the M6 engine range.
keyChanges: LCI facelift across Coupe, Convertible and Gran Coupe
S63B44B introduced with Valvetronic variable valve lift
2015-2018 models received S63T2 (600hp) for Competition Package
Improved cooling and revised turbocharger design
Final M6 production year was 2018 with no direct successor
mainEngines: S63B44B - 4.4L Petrol (M6 Coupe LCI, M6 Convertible LCI, M6 Gran Coupe LCI)
popularModels: BMW M6 Coupe Competition Package LCI
BMW M6 Gran Coupe Competition Package LCI
knownFor: S63T2 is the most reliable S63 variant with reduced turbo failure rates
Oil consumption via valve stem seals still documented
Cooling system overhaul recommended as preventative maintenance at 70k miles
Final M6 models hold their value well among collectors
years.cta: Get quotes for 2015-2018 BMW M6 LCI replacement engines

faq.h2: BMW M6 Engine Replacement - Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: How reliable is the BMW M6 S85 V10 engine really?
items.answer: The S85 V10 is thrilling but maintenance-intensive. With meticulous care, it can achieve high mileage, but rod bearing failure is a well-documented weak point that owners address preventatively.
keyPoints: Rod bearing wear is the most common catastrophic failure, typically between 60k-100k miles
Throttle actuator failure triggers limp mode - a £800-£1,200 fix at a specialist
Regular oil changes with correct 10W-60 oil are absolutely non-negotiable
One documented S85 reached 333,000 miles on original engine with obsessive maintenance
items.cta: Get S85 rod bearing replacement quotes for your BMW M6

items.question: What are the common problems with the BMW M6 S63 twin-turbo V8?
items.answer: The S63 V8 is more reliable than the S85 but has its own issues: turbocharger wear, oil consumption from valve stem seals, and cooling system degradation in the hot-vee design.
keyPoints: Turbocharger failures become common after 70,000 miles, especially with neglected oil changes
Valve stem seal wear causes blue smoke on start-up - replacement costs £1,200-£2,200
Cooling system overhaul (hoses, water pump) is recommended preventative maintenance at 70k miles
Later S63T2 (2015-2018) is the most reliable iteration with revised turbos
items.cta: Compare S63 engine replacement prices for your BMW M6

items.question: How much does a BMW M6 engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: Expect £4,500-£8,500 for a rebuilt S85 V10 (supply only) or £5,500-£11,000 for a rebuilt S63 V8. Supply and fit adds £600-£1,200.
keyPoints: S85 V10 rebuilt engine: £4,500-£8,500 supply only
S63 V8 rebuilt engine: £5,500-£11,000 depending on variant
Dealer new engine prices can exceed £30,000 - specialists are significantly more affordable
Competition Package engines (S63B44C, S63T2) sit at the higher end of the range
items.cta: Get free BMW M6 engine replacement quotes from UK specialists

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in a high-mileage BMW M6?
items.answer: On a car worth £15,000-£25,000, a £5,000-£8,000 engine replacement can be worthwhile if the car is otherwise sound. For very high-mileage cars, weigh the costs carefully.
keyPoints: Preventative rod bearing replacement (£1,200-£2,000) is better value than full engine failure
A well-documented rebuilt engine can increase resale value
Many owners budget £5,000-£10,000 as a "contingency fund" for M6 ownership
BMW-backed warranties are available for cars under 60k miles for around £80/month
items.cta: Calculate your BMW M6 repair vs replace options

items.question: Should I buy a BMW M6 with the S85 V10 or the S63 V8?
items.answer: Choose the S85 V10 for naturally aspirated character and that 8,250rpm scream - but accept higher maintenance. Choose the S63 V8 for more torque, better reliability, and lower running costs.
keyPoints: S85 V10 delivers 507hp and an unforgettable soundtrack but demands meticulous maintenance
S63 V8 offers 560-600hp with significantly more torque and better fuel economy
2015-2018 S63T2 is the most reliable M6 engine option
Pre-2015 S63 engines share some early turbo and bearing concerns
items.cta: Compare S85 vs S63 engine prices for your BMW M6

items.question: Can I fit a BMW M6 engine myself or do I need a specialist?
items.answer: M6 engine replacement is not a DIY job. These are complex, high-performance engines requiring specialist tools, coding knowledge, and experience with M-division powertrains.
keyPoints: S85 and S63 engines require precise timing tools and M-specific diagnostic equipment
ECU coding and immobiliser matching is essential after engine replacement
Specialist fitting costs £600-£1,200 - a fraction of potential DIY mistakes
Supply & fit packages include warranty and old unit disposal
items.cta: Find BMW M6 engine fitting specialists near you

items.question: How often should I change the oil in my BMW M6 to prevent engine failure?
items.answer: Every 6,000 miles or annually - whichever comes first. Extended dealer intervals (15,000+ miles) are a primary cause of rod bearing and turbo failures on both S85 and S63 engines.
keyPoints: S85 requires Castrol Edge 10W-60 - never use cheaper alternatives
S63 requires high-quality 5W-30 or 5W-40 synthetic oil
Oil varnish and sludge buildup is the leading cause of premature S85 failure
The infamous 121,000-mile locked-up S85 had cheap oil and long change intervals
items.cta: Get BMW M6 oil service quotes from specialists

items.question: Does a rebuilt BMW M6 engine come with a warranty?
items.answer: Yes. Every engine supplied through EnginesMarket includes a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty. Specialist rebuilders often offer extended 24-36 month coverage.
keyPoints: Minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty on all supplied engines
Premier rebuilders offer 24-36 month warranties on remanufactured units
Dealer new engines come with 2-year warranty but cost 3-4x more than specialist rebuilt units
Warranty typically covers internal failure but not installation-related issues
items.cta: Compare warrantied BMW M6 engine prices

items.question: What engine codes were fitted to the BMW M6 and which years?
keyPoints: S63B44B - 4.4L V8 with Valvetronic - LCI models (2015-2018)
S63B44C / S63T2 - Competition Package variants (575-600hp)
items.cta: Use our VIN decoder to identify your BMW M6 engine code

items.question: What causes BMW M6 engine failure and how can I prevent it?
items.answer: The top killers are rod bearing wear (S85), turbocharger failure (S63), and oil starvation. Prevention requires strict maintenance, warm-up discipline, and proactive component replacement.
keyPoints: Never exceed 6,000-mile oil change intervals with the correct premium oil
Allow the engine to warm up before exceeding 3,000 RPM - cold revs destroy bearings
Replace S85 rod bearings preventatively at 60k-80k miles (£1,200-£2,000)
Early S63 owners should budget for turbo replacement after 70k miles
items.cta: Get preventative maintenance quotes for your BMW M6
